request.question: Which board game won more awards in 1983, Scotland Yard or Shengguan Tu?

title: Scotland Yard vs. Dr. Mabuse
Stryker of the Yard
Black Museum
Psychopath (1968 film)
Commissioners of Scotland Yard
Scotland Yard (band)
Shengguan Tu
Fantômas contre Scotland Yard
Scotland Yard (board game)
221B Baker Street (board game)
sentences: Scotland Yard vs. Dr Mabuse (German: Scotland Yard jagt Dr. Mabuse , also known as "Scotland Yard Hunts Dr. Mabuse") is a 1963 German crime film directed by Paul May and starring Peter van Eyck.

Stryker of the Yard (A.K.A. Stryker of Scotland Yard and Scotland Yard Cases ) is a 1953 British crime film directed by Arthur Crabtree and starring Clifford Evans, Susan Stephen, Jack Watling and Eliot Makeham.
The Black Museum, or The Crime Museum of Scotland Yard, is a collection of criminal memorabilia kept at New Scotland Yard, headquarters of the Metropolitan Police Service in London, England.
The museum came into existence sometime in 1874, although unofficially.
It was housed at Scotland Yard, and grew from the collection of prisoners' property gathered under the authority of the Prisoners Property Act of 1869.
The act was intended to help the police in their study of crime and criminals.
By 1875, it had become an official museum, although not open to the public, with a police inspector and a police constable assigned to official duty there.
Psychopath (Italian: "Sigpress contro Scotland Yard" , German: "Mister Zehn Prozent - Miezen und Moneten" ) is a 1968 Italian-German film directed by .
The film is about Sigpress (Francisco Martinez Celeiro), a thief who steals jewels which he then returns to the owners in order to collect money as a reward.
He is being tracked by Scotland Yard when he learns that the gem called the Eye of Allah is being escorted from London to Paris.
On attempting to steal the jewel, he finds it's a fake which leads to his own investigation.

The Commissioners of Scotland Yard was the informal name for the Commissioners for the Streets and Wayes, a body of improvement commissioners established in 1662 to manage and regulate various areas relating to streets and traffic in the cities of London and Westminster and the borough of Southwark.
They were appointed under a 1662 Act of the Parliament of England (14 Car.
2 c. 2) which expired in 1679.
The Commissioners' office was attached to that of the Surveyor of the King's Works in Scotland Yard.
Scotland Yard is a pop rock group from Los Angeles, California: Scotland Yard began when Chris Hill began demoing songs in 1989 for a female fronted new wave pop group.
In 1991 Kim Cahill responded to an out of date ad for a singer in a local industry trade magazine.
The edgy pop rock sound from the duo would catch an ear in the Los Angeles and Orange county music scene.

Shengguan Tu (), translated variously as Promoting Officials and Table of Bureaucratic Promotion, is an ancient Chinese board game that originated in the Tang dynasty, with the earliest historical record of a variant of it dating back to 836.

Compared to "Monopoly" and snakes and ladders by contemporary commentators, the game has players take on the roles of mandarins; the objective of the game is to attain the highest possible bureaucratic position, whilst accumulating more prestige and monetary funds than other players.
Historically a gambling game doubling as an educational tool for acquainting Chinese males with the bureaucratic hierarchy, it still enjoys relative popularity nowadays.

Fantômas contre Scotland Yard (] , "Fantomas Against Scotland Yard") is the final installment of a trilogy of films starring Jean Marais as the arch villain with the same name opposite Louis de Funès as the earnest but outclassed commissaire Juve and the journalist Fandor, also played by Marais.
The trilogy was France's humorous answer, starting in 1964, to the James Bond phenomenon that swept the world at around the same time.
The "Fantômas" films became extremely successful in Europe and USSR, and found success even in the United States and Japan where fan websites exist to this day.
Scotland Yard is a board game in which a team of players, as police, cooperate to track down a player controlling a criminal around a board representing the streets of London.
It is named after Scotland Yard, the headquarters of London's Metropolitan Police Service.
"Scotland Yard" is an asymmetric board game, with the detective players cooperatively solving a variant of the pursuit-evasion problem.
The game is published by Ravensburger in most of Europe and Canada and by Milton Bradley in the United States.
It received the "Spiel des Jahres" (Game of the Year) award in 1983.

221B Baker Street: The Master Detective Game is a board game featuring Arthur Conan Doyle's fictional detective Sherlock Holmes and developed by Jay Moriarty (dba Antler Productions) in 1975 and sold by the John N. Hansen Co. in the US since 1977.
The players have to solve cases using the clues provided by visiting locations on the board such as 221B Baker Street, Scotland Yard, Apothecary and Pawn Broker.
The original game has twenty cases, but there are eight expansion sets of 20 cases each for a total of 180 cases available for play.
A new Deluxe Edition of 221 B Baker Street, published by Hansen, has been released in the U.S. in 2016-17.
This Deluxe Edition contains the original 180 Cases plus 20 all-new never-before-solved Cases for a Complete Set of 200 Cases.
The Deluxe Edition also includes all new artwork and collectible Sherlockian metal tokens.
The original game has been licensed for sale in the UK (Gibsons Games), Canada, Australia, New Zealand and Brazil.
Editions of the game published by Gibson have reproduced 40 of the 180 cases of the Hansen edition, but also include 80 new cases.
The Brazil version is titled Scotland Yard and translated in Portuguese.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Which board game won more awards in 1983, Scotland Yard or Shengguan Tu?
hop1_docs: Scotland Yard (board game) | Scotland Yard is a board game in which a team of players, as police, cooperate to track down a player controlling a criminal around a board representing the streets of London.  It is named after Scotland Yard, the headquarters of London's Metropolitan Police Service.  "Scotland Yard" is an asymmetric board game, with the detective players cooperatively solving a variant of the pursuit-evasion problem.  The game is published by Ravensburger in most of Europe and Canada and by Milton Bradley in the United States.  It received the "Spiel des Jahres" (Game of the Year) award in 1983.

Shengguan Tu | Shengguan Tu (), translated variously as Promoting Officials and Table of Bureaucratic Promotion, is an ancient Chinese board game that originated in the Tang dynasty, with the earliest historical record of a variant of it dating back to 836.  Compared to "Monopoly" and snakes and ladders by contemporary commentators, the game has players take on the roles of mandarins; the objective of the game is to attain the highest possible bureaucratic position, whilst accumulating more prestige and monetary funds than other players.  Historically a gambling game doubling as an educational tool for acquainting Chinese males with the bureaucratic hierarchy, it still enjoys relative popularity nowadays.

7 Wonders (board game) | 7 Wonders is a board game created by Antoine Bauza in 2010 and originally published by Repos Production in Belgium.  "7 Wonders" is a card drafting game that is played using three decks of cards featuring depictions of ancient civilizations, military conflicts and commercial activity.  The game is highly regarded, being one of the highest rated games on the board game discussion website BoardGameGeek.  "7 Wonders" has won a total of more than 30 gaming awards, including the inaugural Kennerspiel des Jahres connoisseurs' award in 2011.
Arno Steinwender | is an Austrian board game designer.  In his free time he develops board games, for which he has won several awards.

Zombies!!! | Zombies!!!  is a tile-based strategy board game for two to six players.  "Zombies!!!"  won the 2001 Origins Award for "Best Graphic Presentation of a Board Game", and Zombies!!!  3: Mall Walkers won 2003's Origins Award for "Best Board Game Expansion".
Dao (game) | Dao is a two-player abstract strategy board game, which won the Mensa Select award in 2001.  It comprises a 4x4 playing board with four playing pieces for each player.

DVONN | DVONN is a two-player strategy board game in which the objective is to accumulate pieces in stacks.  It was released in 2001 by Kris Burm as the fourth game of the "GIPF" Project.  "DVONN" won the 2002 International Gamers Award and the "Games" magazine Game of the Year Award in 2003.
response.summary_1: Scotland Yard: passage [1] states it "received the 'Spiel des Jahres' (Game of the Year) award in January 1, 1983." Shengguan Tu: passage [2] describes the game's origin and play but contains no mention of awards. Missing fact/next retrieval: whether Shengguan Tu ever won any awards (and specifically whether it won any awards in 1983); search for "Shengguan Tu awards" or "Shengguan Tu 1983 award" (or authoritative references listing awards for Shengguan Tu).
hop2_docs: Shengguan Tu | Shengguan Tu (), translated variously as Promoting Officials and Table of Bureaucratic Promotion, is an ancient Chinese board game that originated in the Tang dynasty, with the earliest historical record of a variant of it dating back to 836.  Compared to "Monopoly" and snakes and ladders by contemporary commentators, the game has players take on the roles of mandarins; the objective of the game is to attain the highest possible bureaucratic position, whilst accumulating more prestige and monetary funds than other players.  Historically a gambling game doubling as an educational tool for acquainting Chinese males with the bureaucratic hierarchy, it still enjoys relative popularity nowadays.

Hu Tu Tu | Hu Tu Tu is a 1999 Hindi drama directed by Gulzar.  Tabu received a 2000 Filmfare Award nomination for Best Actress for this movie.  Suhasini Mulay won National Film Award for Best Supporting Actress in 1999 for this movie.
Tu Esclavo y Amo | Tu Esclavo y Amo is a studio album released by Lupillo Rivera in 2009.  "Tu Escalvo y Amo" earnerd Rivera a Grammy award for Best Banda Album at the 52nd Annual Grammy Awards.

Nicolás López (director) | Nicolás Javier López Fernández (born 1983) is a Chilean film director, producer, actor and screenwriter.  He is known for the films "Promedio Rojo" and the trilogy "Qué pena tu vida", "Qué pena tu boda" and "Qué pena tu familia".

Tu Olor | "Tu Olor" (English: "Your Scent") is the second single by Wisin & Yandel's album .  A music video was filmed and released.  It received and award for "Urban Song of the Year" at the 2012 ASCAP Awards, which are awarded annually by the American Society of Composers, Authors and Publishers in the United States.

Tu Youyou | Tu Youyou (; born 30 December 1930) is a Chinese pharmaceutical chemist and educator.  She is best known for discovering artemisinin (also known as "qinghaosu") and dihydroartemisinin, used to treat malaria, and which has saved millions of lives.  Tu Youyou's discovery of artemisinin and its treatment of malaria is regarded as a significant breakthrough in 20th century tropical medicine and an important health improvement for people of tropical developing countries in South Asia, Africa, and South America.  For her work, Tu received the 2011 Lasker Award in clinical medicine and the 2015 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine jointly with William C. Campbell and Satoshi Ōmura.  Tu is the first Chinese Nobel laureate in physiology or medicine and the first female citizen of the People's Republic of China to receive a Nobel Prize in any category, as well as the first Chinese person to receive the Lasker Award.  Tu Youyou was born and educated and carried out research exclusively in China.

Tu mi turbi | Tu mi turbi (also known as "You Upset Me" and "You Disturb Me") is a 1983 Italian anthology comedy film written, directed and starred by Roberto Benigni.  It is both the directorial debut of Benigni and the film debut of Nicoletta Braschi.
response.summary_2: Scotland Yard won the Spiel des Jahres in 1983; the retrieved passages about Shengguan Tu contain no mention of any awards, so Scotland Yard won more awards in 1983.
